text: The surgeon begins the low anterior resection for rectal cancer by dividing the inferior mesenteric vein using clips, followed by cutting through the vein. The mesentery is separated from the underlying kidney. The inferior mesenteric artery is isolated and divided using a surgical stapler.

The surgeon enters the pelvis, visualizing the rectum and surrounding pelvic structures. They navigate into the avascular plane above the sacrum, carefully separating tissues from the lateral pelvis and posteriorly from the sacrum and tailbone. The mesorectum is kept intact with the specimen, minimizing bleeding. The bladder is located anteriorly, and the surgeon progresses through the peritoneal reflection into a deeper pelvic space containing significant vessels and nerves.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the rectum, working in the avascular plane behind the rectum near the tailbone. The pelvic floor muscles are visible as red structures, and the rectum is divided from the lower part towards the anal canal and sphincters. The staple line marks the location of the anal canal, with visible manipulation from a finger at the anus.
